title: Marriage and Divorce in Islam and Christianity
subtitle: Contrasting approaches to matrimonial law, consent, and dissolution
summary: Islam and Christianity hold distinct theological and legal frameworks for marriage and divorce. While Islamic law emphasizes contractual consent and allows remarriage under specific conditions, Christian tradition—particularly Catholic and Orthodox—emphasizes sacramental indissolubility. This article examines the scriptural foundations, legal frameworks, and scholarly interpretations within each tradition.

seo.metaTitle: Marriage and Divorce in Islam and Christianity: Scriptural and Legal Perspectives
seo.metaDescription: A comparative analysis of Islamic and Christian approaches to marriage contracts, polygyny, divorce, and remarriage based on Quranic and Biblical sources.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.body: Marriage in Islam and Christianity represents one of the most significant differences in familial and social law between the two traditions. Both religions ground their marriage laws in divine revelation, yet interpret their scriptures in fundamentally different ways. Islam views marriage primarily as a civil contract (nikah) that establishes mutual rights and obligations, while mainstream Christianity—especially Catholicism and Orthodoxy—views marriage as a sacrament, a sacred bond created by God that reflects the covenant between Christ and the Church. These different frameworks lead to divergent approaches to divorce, remarriage, and the rights of both spouses. Understanding these distinctions requires examining the scriptural foundations, scholarly interpretations, and historical development within each tradition.

sections.title: Islamic Marriage (Nikah)
sections.body: In Islamic law, marriage is a civil contract (nikah) based on mutual consent between two competent parties. The Quran emphasizes consent and establishes marriage as a means through which God creates affection and mercy between spouses. The contract involves an ijab (offer) and qabul (acceptance), with the bride's explicit consent being essential. Traditional Islamic jurisprudence requires the presence of witnesses and a mahr (dower)—a gift or payment from the groom to the bride as a matter of right. The Quran outlines principles of marital conduct, rights to maintenance, and proper treatment. Marriage in Islam is considered an act of worship and a significant part of the faith. Islamic law recognizes several forms of marriage dissolution, including talaq (male-initiated divorce), khul'a (female-initiated divorce), and annulment, each with specific conditions and waiting periods. The fact that marriage is a contract rather than a sacrament means it can be dissolved, though Islamic jurisprudence has developed procedures intended to minimize unnecessary divorce.

sections.title: Polygyny in Islam
sections.body: The Quran permits Muslim men to marry up to four wives simultaneously, with specific conditions and obligations. This provision must be understood within its historical context and the requirements attached to it. The verse states that men may marry multiple women only if they fear they cannot do justice among them in maintaining them and treating them fairly. Furthermore, the permission is qualified by the principle of just and equal treatment, and Islamic scholars debate whether perfect equality is practically possible, with many concluding that only equal material provision is obligatory while emotional equality may not be. However, critics point to Quran 4:129, which states that husbands can never achieve full justice and equality among multiple wives even if they strive to do so, and argue that this verse undermines the 4:3 conditional by suggesting the restriction cannot truly be met in practice. Islamic scholars respond that 4:129 refers to emotional equality (which is human nature) rather than material provision, which remains obligatory, and that the Quranic permission is qualified and restricted rather than unrestricted. Additionally, the right to polygyny is one-directional—Islamic law does not recognize a reciprocal right for women to marry multiple husbands. The practical effect of these conditions has led some Islamic legal traditions to restrict or regulate polygyny through judicial requirements, though most Muslim-majority states still permit it with minimal restriction, with only a handful (such as Tunisia) having explicitly banned the practice. Modern Islamic jurisdictions vary widely in their approach: some maintain the right to marry multiple wives with conditions, others require judicial permission, and a few have effectively abolished the practice through legislation. The verse also addresses orphans and remarriage, indicating the historical context of social protection and reestablishing orphaned girls in society after wars and conflicts.

sections.title: Islamic Divorce and Remarriage
sections.body: Islamic law recognizes several forms of divorce, each with distinct procedures and consequences. Talaq (pronouncement of divorce by the husband) is the most common form, though Islamic jurisprudence has developed strict requirements around how and when it is valid. The Quran specifies a waiting period (iddah) of three menstrual cycles during which the couple may reconcile. According to mainstream Islamic jurisprudence, talaq should be pronounced once per period across three separate menstrual cycles to allow for reconciliation. However, historically in some legal traditions, most notably in Hanafi jurisprudence, the practice of pronouncing talaq three times in a single sitting (triple talaq or talaq-e-biddat) was treated as valid, even though jurists themselves considered it sinful and against the spirit of Islamic teaching. This practice, which left women without recourse or reconciliation periods, has become highly controversial in modern times. It has been legislated against in several countries: India reformed its personal status law in 2019, Egypt banned instant triple talaq in 1929, and Pakistan restricted it in 1961. Islamic scholars have responded to this controversy by emphasizing that classical jurists disapproved of instant triple divorce, that it contradicts the Quranic emphasis on reconciliation and kind treatment, and that modern Islamic legal reform to require proper procedure and judicial oversight aligns with both the Quranic intent and classical scholarly disapproval. If divorce becomes final after three pronouncements across three separate periods, remarriage between the same couple requires an intervening marriage to another person and subsequent divorce. This condition, known as tahlil (nikah al-muhallil), is intended to create a barrier to reckless, repeated divorce and hasty reconciliation. The practice has drawn sustained and well-documented criticism, however: Jami at-Tirmidhi 1119 reports the Prophet cursing both the muhallil (the man who marries the divorced woman solely to make her lawful again for her former husband) and the muhallil lahu (the former husband for whom this arrangement is made), and in documented cases the practice has been used to exploit women, reducing them to a legal instrument passed between two men rather than an autonomous party to a genuine marriage. The overwhelming majority of classical jurists across the Sunni schools agree with the substance of this objection: they hold that a marriage contracted with the explicit condition, or prior mutual understanding, that it will end in divorce so as to enable remarriage to the first husband is either invalid or, at minimum, gravely sinful, since it fails the requirement of a genuine marital intent; only a marriage entered into in good faith, with no prior arrangement to divorce, and that subsequently ends for its own independent reasons, satisfies the condition described in 2:230. In other words, mainstream Islamic scholarship treats bad-faith tahlil as itself forbidden rather than as a loophole the law endorses, even though the underlying legal mechanism (an intervening marriage) remains part of classical doctrine. Khul'a is a form of divorce initiated by the wife, typically involving the return of her dower. Islamic law also recognizes other forms of dissolution such as mutual agreement to divorce and annulment in cases where certain conditions of the marriage contract were not fulfilled. Islamic divorce does not eliminate the father's obligation to maintain children, and the mother typically has custody during their early years. The Quran emphasizes that divorce should be handled with kindness and that retained women should be treated according to recognized standards of propriety. Islamic law generally permits remarriage after the waiting period has been completed, though the waiting period has different durations for widows and divorced women, and additional conditions may apply in some legal schools.

sections.title: Christian Marriage as Sacrament
sections.body: Christian theology, particularly in Catholic and Orthodox traditions, understands marriage as a sacrament—a sacred bond established by God through which Christ is mysteriously present. This sacramental understanding goes beyond contract; it views marriage as a profound sign and instrument of divine grace. The marriage covenant in Christian theology mirrors the covenant between Christ and the Church, as reflected in New Testament passages. Jesus's teachings emphasize the permanence and indissolubility of marriage, drawing believers back to God's original design in creation. In Catholic theology, the sacrament of matrimony grants the couple themselves as ministers of the sacrament, and valid marriage requires the free consent of both parties, sufficient use of reason, and absence of impediments. The Eastern Orthodox tradition emphasizes marriage as a mystery (sacrament) that theosis (deification) occurs through mutual love and sacrifice. Both traditions developed detailed canonical law regarding impediments to marriage, proper form requirements, and conditions for annulment. Unlike Islamic law, which allows divorce and remarriage under specified conditions, Christian sacramental theology maintains that a valid consummated marriage cannot be dissolved by human authority—only death ends the bond. The Second Vatican Council's documents affirm that marriage is not merely a means of procreation but also an end in itself, a communion of love between spouses.

sections.title: Christian Indissolubility and Divorce
sections.body: The Christian teaching on divorce's prohibition stems from Jesus's words in the Gospel. In Matthew 19, Jesus is questioned about whether divorce is permitted for any reason (as some Jewish schools of that era taught). Jesus responds by pointing back to creation, emphasizing that God made humans male and female and instituted the union of man and woman. He states that what God has joined together, no one should separate. When pressed about why Moses allowed divorce, Jesus explains that this was permitted only because of the hardness of human hearts, but it was not so from the beginning. Jesus then states that whoever divorces his wife (except for sexual immorality) and marries another commits adultery. This teaching was revolutionary in its cultural context, as it elevated the status of women by making them equal partners in the covenant rather than property that could be easily dismissed. The exception clause 'except for sexual immorality' (or porneia in Greek) has been interpreted differently by Christian traditions: some understand it as permitting divorce and remarriage in cases of adultery, while others argue it refers to a dissolution of the marriage bond itself (not permission for remarriage). Paul, writing to the Corinthians, reinforces the prohibition on divorce while acknowledging difficult situations involving unbelieving spouses. In cases where a believing spouse is abandoned by an unbelieving partner, Paul states that the believing spouse is not bound, offering what some interpret as a limited exception to the prohibition. The Catholic Church has developed the doctrine of annulment, which declares that no valid sacramental marriage existed in the first place due to some impediment or defective consent, thus distinguishing between divorce (dissolution of a valid marriage) and annulment (declaration that a valid marriage never existed). However, a frequently raised critique observes that the sharp rise in annulments granted after Vatican II—particularly in the United States where church tribunals historically grant the large majority of annulment petitions—has led many observers, including some Catholics, to view the annulment process as functioning as a legal fiction equivalent to divorce rather than as a substantively different outcome. The Church responds to this critique by reaffirming that annulment requires genuine investigation into whether all conditions for a valid sacramental marriage were present, that the increase in annulments reflects greater awareness of impediments and defective consent rather than a lowering of standards, and that the process, while reform-worthy, remains theologically and legally distinct from divorce because it establishes that no valid marriage bond was created in the first place.

sections.title: Protestant Approaches to Divorce
sections.body: Protestant Reformation theology introduced significant variations in the Christian approach to divorce and remarriage. Martin Luther argued that, while divorce was contrary to God's design, it was permissible in certain cases, particularly adultery and abandonment, and that remarriage was permissible after such divorce. Many Protestant traditions adopted the exception clause from Matthew's Gospel more liberally than Catholic interpretation, permitting divorce and remarriage in cases of adultery or desertion. Over time, Protestant denominations developed diverse positions. Some traditions maintain strict prohibitions similar to Catholic teaching, while others permit divorce for adultery and abandonment, and still others have adopted more permissive approaches allowing divorce for irreconcilable differences. The grounds for divorce permitted vary significantly among Protestant denominations, reflecting different theological interpretations of Jesus's teachings. Unlike the Catholic annulment process, which attempts to maintain the principle of indissolubility by declaring that a valid marriage never existed, Protestant denominations that permit divorce accept that valid marriages can be dissolved. This represents a fundamental theological difference: Protestants generally view marriage as a covenant that can be broken and dissolved under certain circumstances, while Catholics maintain that a valid sacramental marriage remains binding regardless of circumstances. Modern evangelical Christianity shows further variation, with some denominations emphasizing complementarian views of gender roles (which may affect interpretation of divorce texts) while others emphasize egalitarian approaches.

sections.title: Comparative Legal Frameworks
sections.body: The legal frameworks governing marriage and divorce in Islam and Christianity reflect their theological foundations. Islamic law, derived from the Quran, Hadith, and scholarly consensus, treats marriage as a legally binding but dissolvable contract. This framework allows for several mechanisms of divorce, each with procedural requirements designed to prevent hasty dissolution and protect the rights of both spouses and children. The Islamic system is characterized by specificity: it defines clear grounds for divorce, establishes waiting periods for reflection and possible reconciliation, and mandates maintenance payments and custody arrangements. Islamic jurisprudence emphasizes that divorce, while permissible, is discouraged, a principle supported by a widely cited though contested hadith attributed to Ibn Umar that refers to divorce as the most disliked of permissible things; hadith scholars have graded this hadith's chain of transmission as weak (da'if) due to breaks in its transmission. Christian legal frameworks, particularly Catholic canon law, developed around the principle of indissolubility. Canon law specifies impediments to valid marriage and grounds for annulment but does not recognize divorce as a dissolution of a valid marriage. The annulment process involves investigating whether all conditions for a valid sacramental marriage were present and whether proper consent existed. Protestant legal systems, where Christianity is the established religion, typically recognize divorce as permissible under certain circumstances, though the specific grounds vary. In modern secular jurisdictions, many countries have adopted civil divorce systems that are secular rather than based on religious law, though they may still be influenced by Christian historical traditions (particularly regarding fault-based or no-fault divorce grounds). Conversely, some Muslim-majority countries incorporate Islamic divorce provisions into their civil law, though with significant modern modifications and protections for women's rights.

sections.title: Women's Rights and Protections
sections.body: Both Islamic and Christian traditions have developed provisions intended to protect women's rights within marriage and in the event of dissolution. In Islamic law, a woman's right to dower (mahr) is recognized as a matter of right and obligation, establishing her financial security within the marriage. Islamic law grants women the right to khul'a (divorce initiated by the wife, typically involving return of dower), and modern Islamic jurisdictions have expanded this right. A woman's right to maintenance (nafaqah) from her husband is explicitly mandated, and this obligation continues even after divorce for the period of the waiting period and beyond if she has children in her custody. Islamic inheritance law gives women specific rights, though not equal in quantity to men's shares (reflecting different financial obligations in Islamic law). The Quran explicitly commands kind treatment of wives, and Islamic jurisprudence has developed provisions protecting women from arbitrary treatment. However, classical Islamic law placed the right to pronounce talaq primarily with men, a feature that modern Islamic legal reforms in many countries have sought to modify through requiring judicial involvement or expanding women's unilateral divorce rights. In Christian tradition, particularly after the Reformation and modern development, marriage is understood as a partnership of equals. The Catholic Church teaches that husband and wife have mutual rights and duties, rejecting patriarchal models that treat women as subordinate. Jesus's teaching on indissolubility functioned historically to elevate women's status by preventing arbitrary abandonment and establishing their equal partnership in the covenant. However, Christian teaching also developed alongside cultural patriarchy, and women's rights to property, inheritance, and voice in society were historically limited in Christian-majority cultures. Modern Christian communities have increasingly emphasized the equality and dignity of women within marriage, though interpretations remain diverse.

sections.title: Age of Marriage and Capacity to Consent
sections.body: A frequently raised objection concerns the absence, in the Quran and classical Islamic jurisprudence, of a fixed minimum chronological age for marriage. The objection is sharpened by reports in Sahih al-Bukhari 5134 and Sahih Muslim 1422b stating that Aisha bint Abi Bakr was six or seven years old at the time of her marriage contract to the Prophet Muhammad and nine at its consummation. Critics argue that this precedent has been invoked historically, and in some places still today, to justify child marriage, and that classical jurisprudence's reliance on physical puberty (bulugh) rather than a fixed age as the threshold for a valid, consummated marriage leaves minors without meaningful protection or genuine capacity to consent. Mainstream Islamic scholarly responses take several forms. Many scholars hold that this historical detail reflects the norms of 7th-century Arabia rather than a timeless prescription, and point out that some hadith specialists and historians have separately questioned the reported age on internal chronological grounds (comparing it with the recorded age of Aisha's sister, Asma). Others argue that the Quran's requirement of rushd—mature, sound judgment—before a person may be entrusted with their own property (Quran 4:6) implies that Islamic law's underlying concern is substantive capacity, not mere physical maturity, and that nothing in the Quran or Sunnah prevents the state from legislating a minimum marriage age in the interest of public welfare (maslaha); the vast majority of contemporary Islamic scholars and legal authorities support such legislation. In practice, nearly every Muslim-majority country has adopted a statutory minimum marriage age in civil law, commonly 18 for both sexes (for example Tunisia, Turkey, and Indonesia since 2019), though some jurisdictions retain lower ages or judicial-exception provisions that remain a live area of reform advocacy by both secular and religious voices. For comparative context, premodern Christian canon law set its own formal minimum ages for marriage at 12 for girls and 14 for boys—an age of legal 'discretion' rather than full physical or emotional maturity—a standard that went largely unchanged until the modern era. Child marriage under both premodern religious legal frameworks is now widely regarded, including by many religious authorities within each tradition, as incompatible with contemporary understandings of consent, capacity, and child welfare.

sections.title: Historical Context and Development
sections.body: The development of Islamic and Christian marriage law occurred within specific historical and cultural contexts. Islam emerged in 7th-century Arabia, where pre-Islamic marriage practices ranged from monogamy to forms of marriage that provided no protection to women or their children. The Quranic regulations on marriage established rights for women previously denied in Arabian society, including the right to dower, maintenance, and consent in marriage. The progressive revelation model in Islamic theology suggests that Quranic permissions and prohibitions were revealed gradually in response to specific social conditions, with the aim of establishing justice and protection. Christian marriage law developed through different phases. Early Christianity, influenced by Paul's letters and early church teachings, emphasized celibacy as an ideal for those called to it, while permitting marriage for those without the gift of celibacy. By the medieval period, the Christian Church had developed marriage as a sacrament with specific theological significance. The indissolubility teaching, while rooted in Jesus's words, was codified and developed through centuries of theological reflection and canon law. The Reformation challenged some aspects of this development, leading to different Protestant approaches. Modern developments in both traditions reflect engagement with changing social conditions, women's rights movements, and secular legal systems. Islamic legal reforms in countries like Tunisia, Morocco, and Egypt have significantly modified traditional divorce law, introducing judicial involvement, establishing grounds for women-initiated divorce, and setting age requirements. Christian denominations have similarly engaged with modern understandings of marriage, psychology, and women's equality, leading to diverse modern positions even within single traditions.

sections.title: Scriptural Foundation
sections.body: Both Islamic and Christian approaches to marriage and divorce are grounded in scriptural texts that are interpreted and developed through centuries of scholarly tradition. The Quran establishes fundamental principles for marriage: mutual affection and mercy between spouses, consent-based union, the right to dower, and procedures for divorce with mandatory waiting periods. The Quran addresses various scenarios including orphans, widows, divorced women, breastfeeding arrangements, and marital responsibilities. Christian scripture, particularly the Gospels and Paul's letters, establish Jesus's teaching on the permanence of marriage and the equality of the marriage covenant. The New Testament also addresses practical questions about marriage, celibacy, and conduct within marriage. Both scriptures developed through interpretation and application to specific situations, a process that continues to generate legitimate scholarly and theological debate within each tradition. Islamic scholarship developed elaborate jurisprudential systems (madhabs or schools of law) that applied Quranic principles to new situations and developed detailed procedures around divorce. Christian scholarship similarly developed through patristic theology, scholasticism, and modern biblical studies. The relationship between scriptural text and legal/ethical application differs between the traditions: Islamic jurisprudence emphasizes the precise meaning of Quranic language and the application of qiyas (analogical reasoning), while Christian theology emphasizes the principle and spirit of Jesus's teaching, allowing for application to diverse contexts.

sections.title: Underlying Ethical Principles
sections.body: Beneath the specific legal provisions in Islamic and Christian law lie fundamental ethical principles that guide each tradition's approach to marriage and divorce. Islamic ethics emphasize justice (adl), mercy (rahmah), and the establishment of rights and obligations. The requirement to treat wives with kindness, to pay dower, and to maintain them reflects a framework of justice requiring the stronger party (typically the man, economically) to bear greater obligations. The procedures around divorce—waiting periods, right to reconciliation, maintenance obligations—reflect principles of mercy and the presumption against dissolution. At the same time, Islamic ethics recognize human weakness and the reality that some marriages cannot continue; the permissibility of divorce reflects realistic recognition of human nature. Christian ethics emphasize covenant, self-sacrificial love (agape), and the transformation of human relationships through grace. The Christian teaching on indissolubility is rooted in the understanding that marriage reflects the covenant between Christ and the Church, a covenant that is permanent and unconditional. The teaching emphasizes faithfulness, sacrifice, and working through difficulties rather than abandoning them. Christian ethics also emphasize mutual submission, equality, and the dignity of the person, principles that have been developed significantly in modern Christian theology. Both traditions grapple with the tension between ideals and human reality: Islam permits divorce as a realistic option while discouraging it, while Christianity emphasizes permanence while developing annulment as a way to address situations where genuine marriage never occurred. These different ethical frameworks lead to different legal structures, but both traditions attempt to balance principles of justice, compassion, and protection for vulnerable parties, particularly children and abandoned spouses.

quranVerses.arabic: وَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تُقْسِطُوا۟ فِى ٱلْيَتَٰمَىٰ فَٱنكِحُوا۟ مَا طَابَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ مَثْنَىٰ وَثُلَٰثَ وَرُبَٰعَ ۖ فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا۟ فَوَٰحِدَةً أَوْ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَٰنُكُمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ أَدْنَىٰٓ أَلَّا تَعُولُوا۟
quranVerses.translation: And if you fear that you will not deal justly with the orphan girls, then marry those that please you of [other] women, two or three or four. But if you fear that you will not be just, then [marry only] one or those your right hand possesses. That is more suitable that you may not incline [to injustice].
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: فَإِن طَلَّقَهَا فَلَا تَحِلُّ لَهُۥ مِنۢ بَعْدُ حَتَّىٰ تَنكِحَ زَوْجًا غَيْرَهُۥ ۗ فَإِن طَلَّقَهَا فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْهِمَآ أَن يَتَرَاجَعَآ إِن ظَنَّآ أَن يُقِيمَا حُدُودَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَتِلْكَ حُدُودُ ٱللَّهِ يُبَيِّنُهَا لِقَوْمٍۢ يَعْلَمُونَ
quranVerses.translation: And if he has divorced her [for the third time], then she is not lawful to him afterward until [after] she marries a husband other than him. And if the latter husband divorces her [or dies], there is no blame upon the woman and her former husband for returning to each other if they think that they can keep [within] the limits of Allah. These are the limits of Allah, which He makes clear to a people who know.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَإِذَا طَلَّقْتُمُ ٱلنِّسَآءَ فَبَلَغْنَ أَجَلَهُنَّ فَأَمْسِكُوهُنَّ بِمَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ سَرِّحُوهُنَّ بِمَعْرُوفٍۢ ۚ وَلَا تُمْسِكُوهُنَّ ضِرَارًۭا لِّتَعْتَدُوا۟ ۚ وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ فَقَدْ ظَلَمَ نَفْسَهُۥ ۚ وَلَا تَتَّخِذُوٓا۟ ءَايَٰتِ ٱللَّهِ هُزُوًۭا ۚ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَمَآ أَنزَلَ عَلَيْكُم مِّنَ ٱلْكِتَٰبِ وَٱلْحِكْمَةِ يَعِظُكُم بِهِۦ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌۭ
quranVerses.translation: And when you divorce women and they have [nearly] fulfilled their term, either retain them according to acceptable terms or release them according to acceptable terms, and do not keep them, intending harm, to transgress [against them]. And whoever does that has certainly wronged himself. And do not take the verses of Allah in jest. And remember the favor of Allah upon you and what has been revealed to you of the Book and wisdom by which He instructs you. And fear Allah and know that Allah is Knowing of all things.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَإِذَا طَلَّقْتُمُ ٱلنِّسَآءَ فَبَلَغْنَ أَجَلَهُنَّ فَلَا تَعْضُلُوهُنَّ أَن يَنكِحْنَ أَزْوَٰجَهُنَّ إِذَا تَرَٰضَوْا۟ بَيْنَهُم بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۗ ذَٰلِكَ يُوعَظُ بِهِۦ مَن كَانَ مِنكُمْ يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْءَاخِرِ ۗ ذَٰلِكُمْ أَزْكَىٰ لَكُمْ وَأَطْهَرُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ
quranVerses.translation: And when you divorce women and they have fulfilled their term, do not prevent them from remarrying their [former] husbands if they agree among themselves on an acceptable basis. That is instructed to whoever of you believes in Allah and the Last Day. That is better for you and purer, and Allah knows and you know not.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: ۞ وَٱلْوَٰلِدَٰتُ يُرْضِعْنَ أَوْلَٰدَهُنَّ حَوْلَيْنِ كَامِلَيْنِ ۖ لِمَنْ أَرَادَ أَن يُتِمَّ ٱلرَّضَاعَةَ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱلْمَوْلُودِ لَهُۥ رِزْقُهُنَّ وَكِسْوَتُهُنَّ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۚ لَا تُكَلَّفُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا ۚ لَا تُضَآرَّ وَٰلِدَةٌۢ بِوَلَدِهَا وَلَا مَوْلُودٌۭ لَّهُۥ بِوَلَدِهِۦ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱلْوَارِثِ مِثْلُ ذَٰلِكَ ۗ فَإِنْ أَرَادَا فِصَالًا عَن تَرَاضٍۢ مِّنْهُمَا وَتَشَاوُرٍۢ فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْهِمَا ۗ وَإِنْ أَرَدتُّمْ أَن تَسْتَرْضِعُوٓا۟ أَوْلَٰدَكُمْ فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذَا سَلَّمْتُم مَّآ ءَاتَيْتُم بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۗ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌۭ
quranVerses.translation: Mothers may breastfeed their children two complete years for whoever wishes to complete the nursing [period]. Upon the father is the mothers' provision and their clothing according to what is acceptable. No person is charged with more than his capacity. No mother should be harmed through her child, and no father through his child. And upon the [father's] heir is [a duty] like that [of the father]. And if they both desire weaning through mutual consent from both of them and consultation, there is no blame upon either of them. And if you wish to have your children nursed by a substitute, there is no blame upon you as long as you give payment according to what is acceptable. And fear Allah and know that Allah is Seeing of what you do.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَٱلَّذِينَ يُتَوَفَّوْنَ مِنكُمْ وَيَذَرُونَ أَزْوَٰجًۭا يَتَرَبَّصْنَ بِأَنفُسِهِنَّ أَرْبَعَةَ أَشْهُرٍۢ وَعَشْرًۭا ۖ فَإِذَا بَلَغْنَ أَجَلَهُنَّ فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِيمَا فَعَلْنَ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِنَّ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌۭ
quranVerses.translation: And those who are taken in death among you and leave wives behind - they, [the wives, shall] wait four months and ten [days]. And when they have fulfilled their term, then there is no blame upon you for what they do with themselves in an acceptable manner. And Allah is [fully] Acquainted with what you do.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِيمَا عَرَّضْتُم بِهِۦ مِنْ خِطْبَةِ ٱلنِّسَآءِ أَوْ أَكْنَنتُمْ فِىٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ ۚ عَلِمَ ٱللَّهُ أَنَّكُمْ سَتَذْكُرُونَهُنَّ وَلَٰكِن لَّا تُوَاعِدُوهُنَّ سِرًّا إِلَّآ أَن تَقُولُوا۟ قَوْلًۭا مَّعْرُوفًۭا ۚ وَلَا تَعْزِمُوا۟ عُقْدَةَ ٱلنِّكَاحِ حَتَّىٰ يَبْلُغَ ٱلْكِتَٰبُ أَجَلَهُۥ ۚ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا فِىٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ فَٱحْذَرُوهُ ۚ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌۭ
quranVerses.translation: There is no blame upon you for that to which you [indirectly] allude concerning a proposal to women or for what you conceal within yourselves. Allah knows that you will have them in mind. But do not promise them secretly except for saying a proper saying. And do not determine to undertake a marriage contract until the decreed period reaches its end. And know that Allah knows what is within yourselves, so beware of Him. And know that Allah is Forgiving and Forbearing.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: لَّا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِن طَلَّقْتُمُ ٱلنِّسَآءَ مَا لَمْ تَمَسُّوهُنَّ أَوْ تَفْرِضُوا۟ لَهُنَّ فَرِيضَةًۭ ۚ وَمَتِّعُوهُنَّ عَلَى ٱلْمُوسِعِ قَدَرُهُۥ وَعَلَى ٱلْمُقْتِرِ قَدَرُهُۥ مَتَٰعًۢا بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۖ حَقًّا عَلَى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ
quranVerses.translation: There is no blame upon you if you divorce women you have not touched nor specified for them an obligation. But give them [a gift of] compensation - the wealthy according to his capability and the poor according to his capability - a provision according to what is acceptable, a duty upon the doers of good.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَلَن تَسْتَطِيعُوٓا۟ أَن تَعْدِلُوا۟ بَيْنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ وَلَوْ حَرَصْتُمْ ۖ فَلَا تَمِيلُوا۟ كُلَّ ٱلْمَيْلِ فَتَذَرُوهَا كَٱلْمُعَلَّقَةِ ۚ وَإِن تُصْلِحُوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ غَفُورًۭا رَّحِيمًۭا
quranVerses.translation: And you will never be able to be equal [in feeling] between wives, even if you should strive [to do so]. So do not incline completely [toward one] and leave another hanging. And if you amend [your affairs] and fear Allah - then indeed, Allah is ever Forgiving and Merciful.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَٱبْتَلُوا۟ ٱلْيَتَٰمَىٰ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا بَلَغُوا۟ ٱلنِّكَاحَ فَإِنْ ءَانَسْتُم مِّنْهُمْ رُشْدًۭا فَٱدْفَعُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِمْ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ ۖ وَلَا تَأْكُلُوهَآ إِسْرَافًۭا وَبِدَارًا أَن يَكْبَرُوا۟ ۚ وَمَن كَانَ غَنِيًّۭا فَلْيَسْتَعْفِفْ ۖ وَمَن كَانَ فَقِيرًۭا فَلْيَأْكُلْ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۚ فَإِذَا دَفَعْتُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ فَأَشْهِدُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ حَسِيبًۭا
quranVerses.translation: And test the orphans [in their abilities] until they reach marriageable age. Then if you perceive in them sound judgement, release their property to them. And do not consume it excessively and quickly, [anticipating] that they will grow up. And whoever, [when acting as guardian], is self-sufficient should refrain [from taking a fee]; and whoever is poor - let him take according to what is acceptable. Then when you release their property to them, bring witnesses upon them. And sufficient is Allah as Accountant.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

bibleVerses.text: Pharisees came to him, testing him, and saying, “Is it lawful for a man to divorce his wife for any reason?”

He answered, “Haven’t you read that he who made them from the beginning made them male and female, and said, ‘For this cause a man shall leave his father and mother, and shall be joined to his wife; and the two shall become one flesh?’ So that they are no more two, but one flesh. What therefore God has joined together, don’t let man tear apart.”

They asked him, “Why then did Moses command us to give her a bill of divorce, and divorce her?” He said to them, “Moses, because of the hardness of your hearts, allowed you to divorce your wives, but from the beginning it has not been so. I tell you that whoever divorces his wife, except for sexual immorality, and marries another, commits adultery; and he who marries her when she is divorced commits adultery.”

His disciples said to him, “If this is the case of the man with his wife, it is not expedient to marry.” But he said to them, “Not all men can receive this saying, but those to whom it is given. For there are eunuchs who were born that way from their mother’s womb, and there are eunuchs who were made eunuchs by men; and there are eunuchs who made themselves eunuchs for the Kingdom of Heaven’s sake. He who is able to receive it, let him receive it.”

Now concerning the things about which you wrote to me: it is good for a man not to touch a woman. But, because of sexual immoralities, let each man have his own wife, and let each woman have her own husband.

Let the husband give his wife the affection owed her, and likewise also the wife her husband. The wife doesn’t have authority over her own body, but the husband. Likewise also the husband doesn’t have authority over his own body, but the wife. Don’t deprive one another, unless it is by consent for a season, that you may give yourselves to fasting and prayer, and may be together again, that Satan doesn’t tempt you because of your lack of self-control.

But to the married I command—not I, but the Lord—that the wife not leave her husband (but if she departs, let her remain unmarried, or else be reconciled to her husband), and that the husband not leave his wife.

But to the rest I—not the Lord—say, if any brother has an unbelieving wife, and she is content to live with him, let him not leave her. The woman who has an unbelieving husband, and he is content to live with her, let her not leave her husband. For the unbelieving husband is sanctified in the wife, and the unbelieving wife is sanctified in the husband. Otherwise your children would be unclean, but now they are holy. Yet if the unbeliever departs, let there be separation. The brother or the sister is not under bondage in such cases, but God has called us in peace.

Now concerning virgins, I have no commandment from the Lord, but I give my judgment as one who has obtained mercy from the Lord to be trustworthy. Therefore I think that because of the distress that is on us, that it’s good for a man to remain as he is. Are you bound to a wife? Don’t seek to be freed. Are you free from a wife? Don’t seek a wife. But if you marry, you have not sinned. If a virgin marries, she has not sinned. Yet such will have oppression in the flesh, and I want to spare you.

faq.question: Can Muslims marry more than one wife? What are the conditions?
faq.answer: Islamic law permits Muslim men to marry up to four wives simultaneously, as stated in Quran 4:3. However, the verse attaches significant conditions: the man must fear he cannot deal justly with orphan girls (the historical context), and he must be able to maintain each wife fairly and treat them equally. The verse states that if he fears he cannot be just, then he should marry only one. Islamic scholars interpret 'justice' to refer primarily to financial maintenance and material treatment, though the practical application of these conditions has led many Islamic jurisdictions to restrict or effectively prohibit polygyny through legislation or judicial requirements.

faq.question: Is divorce permitted in Christianity?
faq.answer: This depends on the Christian tradition. The Catholic and Orthodox Churches teach that a valid, consummated sacramental marriage cannot be dissolved, though they recognize annulment (a declaration that a valid marriage never existed). Most Protestant denominations permit divorce under certain circumstances, commonly including adultery and abandonment, though positions vary widely. Some Protestant traditions maintain strict prohibitions on divorce and remarriage, while others have become more permissive. Jesus's teaching in Matthew 19 is interpreted differently by these traditions, with some emphasizing the exception clause for sexual immorality and others focusing on the general principle of permanence.

faq.question: What is khul'a in Islamic law?
faq.answer: Khul'a is a form of divorce initiated by the wife, typically involving the return of her dower (mahr) or other compensation to the husband. Unlike talaq (husband-initiated divorce), which the husband can pronounce unilaterally, khul'a requires mutual agreement. Islamic law recognizes khul'a as a legitimate means for a woman to exit a marriage if she is unhappy. Modern Islamic legal reforms in many countries have expanded women's divorce rights, making khul'a more accessible and, in some jurisdictions, establishing judicial grounds for women-initiated divorce beyond the traditional khul'a framework.

faq.question: What is the iddah (waiting period) in Islamic law?
faq.answer: The iddah is a mandatory waiting period after divorce or widowhood during which a woman must wait before remarrying. For divorced women, the iddah typically consists of three menstrual cycles (approximately three months), during which reconciliation with the former husband is possible. This waiting period serves multiple purposes: it allows time for reflection and potential reconciliation, establishes clear paternity if pregnancy occurs, and provides the woman with a period of support. For widows, the iddah lasts four months and ten days. The Quran specifies these periods in 2:228, 2:234, and related verses.

faq.question: How do Christian and Islamic approaches to marital indissolubility differ?
faq.answer: Catholic and Orthodox Christianity teach that a valid sacramental marriage is indissoluble—it cannot be dissolved by human action. This is based on Jesus's teaching that what God has joined together should not be separated. However, the Church recognizes annulment as a declaration that no valid marriage existed in the first place. Islam, by contrast, permits divorce as a legal remedy when the marriage cannot continue, though Islamic jurisprudence has developed procedures (waiting periods, requirements for just cause, obligations to reconcile) that discourage hasty divorce. Islamic law views marriage as a contract that can be dissolved under certain conditions, while Christian theology views a valid marriage as a permanent covenant.

faq.question: What does the Bible teach about divorce and remarriage?
faq.answer: Jesus's core teaching, found in Matthew 19 and parallel passages, emphasizes the permanence of marriage, stating that what God has joined together should not be separated. He indicates that Moses permitted divorce because of 'hardness of hearts' but that it was not so from the beginning. Jesus states that whoever divorces and remarries commits adultery, with an exception clause for 'sexual immorality' (porneia in Greek). Paul, writing to the Corinthians, commands that spouses should not separate, but adds that if an unbelieving spouse departs, the believing spouse is not bound. Different Christian traditions interpret these passages differently: some allow divorce only for adultery, others for abandonment as well, and still others have adopted more permissive grounds.

furtherReading.title: The Islamic Law of Personal Status
furtherReading.author: Jamal J. Nasir
furtherReading.note: Comprehensive examination of Islamic family law including marriage, divorce, and related matters with comparative analysis of different legal schools

furtherReading.title: Succession in the Muslim Family
furtherReading.author: Noel James Coulson
furtherReading.note: Legal and historical analysis of Islamic family law covering marriage, divorce, inheritance, and succession across various jurisdictions and legal schools

furtherReading.title: An Introduction to Canon Law
furtherReading.author: James A. Coriden
furtherReading.note: Comprehensive introduction to Catholic canon law with detailed treatment of sacramental marriage, impediments, and annulment procedures
